coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence immunoassay based on nitrogen doped carbon dots for the detection of human nuclear matrix protein NMP22 as biomarker for early stage diagnosis of bladder cancer
coredata.dc:creator: Othman, Hazha Omar
Salehnia, Foad
Hosseini, Morteza
Hassan, Rebwar
Faizullah, Azad
Ganjali, Mohammad Reza
coredata.dc:description: A selective, fast acting and eco–friendly fluorescence immunoassay technique based on Nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) is proposed for the detection of Nuclear Matrix Protein 22 (NMP22, antigen). Via the hydrothermal method and with the use of citric acid and urea, we synthesized NCDs with high quantum yield. Monoclonal antibodies (mAb, antibody) were labeled with NCDs through the EDC–NHS amidization technique and, in order to carry out the immunoreaction between the antigen and antibody, the resulting NCDs –mAb conjugates were then incubated with a small amount of NMP22). The established immunocomplex on the carboxylated NCDs led to the quenching of the fluorescence intensity. Under optimal conditions, a linear correlation between the change in fluorescence intensity and NMP22 concentration in the range of 1.3–16.3 ng/mL was observed and a detection limit of 0.047 ng/mL (47 pg/mL) was attained. This detection method was successfully able to determine NMP22 in human urine samples, with recoveries ranging from 96.50% to 103.61%. These results provide ample proof regarding the potential use of NCDs as fluorescence labels in immunoassays.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Immunoassay
Nitrogen doped carbon dots
Nuclear matrix protein 22 (NMP22)
Fluorescence

Fluorescence immunoassay for human NMP22 assessment
The conjugation of antibodies on NCDs
Optimal conditions for human NMP22 detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the detection mechanism of NMP22 using carbon dots.
(a) TEM image of NCDs, (b)UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra of N–CD,(c) FT–IR spectra of N–CD, (d) Energy Dispersive X–ray (EDX) spectra of NCDs.

The conjugation of NCDs with monoclonal antibody (a) The FT–IR spectra of mAb, NCDs, NCDs –mAb;(b) The fluorescence spectra of NCDs before and after conjugation (the exatation wavelength was set at 300 nm.
(a) Effect of NCDs –mAb concentration on the fluorescence Intensity of the system. (b) effect of pH value on the Intensity of the system. (c) Stability of the system for detection of NMP22.
Fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs –mAb–NMP22. NMP22 concentrations: 0.2–16.3 ng/ mL.
The comparison with other immunoassay methods for NMP 22.
Study the effect of different interferences on detection of NMP22 by NCDs.
Recovery tests of NMP22 in the human urine samples.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence immunoassay based on nitrogen doped carbon dots for the detection of human nuclear matrix protein NMP22 as biomarker for early stage diagnosis of bladder cancer

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, College of Science, Salahaddin University–Erbil, Iraq

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Biosensor Research Center, Endocrinology and Metabolism Molecular–Cellular Sciences Institute, Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran 1417614418, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Endocrinology and Metabolism Molecular–Cellular Sciences Institute

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: dBiosensor Research Center, Endocrinology and Metabolism Molecular–Cellular Sciences Institute, Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran 1417614418, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A selective, fast acting and eco–friendly fluorescence immunoassay technique based on Nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) is proposed for the detection of Nuclear Matrix Protein 22 (NMP22, antigen). Via the hydrothermal method and with the use of citric acid and urea, we synthesized NCDs with high quantum yield. Monoclonal antibodies (mAb, antibody) were labeled with NCDs through the EDC–NHS amidization technique and, in order to carry out the immunoreaction between the antigen and antibody, the resulting NCDs –mAb conjugates were then incubated with a small amount of NMP22). The established immunocomplex on the carboxylated NCDs led to the quenching of the fluorescence intensity. Under optimal conditions, a linear correlation between the change in fluorescence intensity and NMP22 concentration in the range of 1.3–16.3 ng/mL was observed and a detection limit of 0.047 ng/mL (47 pg/mL) was attained. This detection method was successfully able to determine NMP22 in human urine samples, with recoveries ranging from 96.50% to 103.61%. These results provide ample proof regarding the potential use of NCDs as fluorescence labels in immunoassays.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a novel category of zero–dimensional nanomaterials in the carbon family [1,2]. As carbon is abundantly present as a basic element in all organisms, carbon nanomaterials are privileged with commonly having low or no toxicity to living organisms, along with being cost effective [3]. These nanoparticles are also bio–compatible and environmentally friendly, demonstrate high chemical and photostability, and can easily form bioconjugates [4,5]. It is noteworthy to mention that the preparation method of CQDs has several advantages including the need for a simple apparatus, mild reaction conditions, low cost and its potential for large–scale fabrication. These positive characteristics make CQDs a promising nanoparticle for biomedical and clinical use [6–11]. So far, a variety of methods have been proposed for CQD production. Depending of their synthetic sequence, these methods can be further divided into top–down and bottom–up approaches [12]. In a top–down process, CQDs are corroded from a carbon source or peeled from carbon nanoparticles via methods such as laser ablation [13,14], electrochemical oxidation [15] and arc discharge [16]. Although in these methods, difficulties regarding the complete breakdown of the carbon skeleton from the original carbon source into carbon nanoparticles can be limiting. In a bottom–up approach, techniques such as burning, heating or microwave are used to attain a carbon precursor, which is then used to generate CQDs via chemical oxidation [17], pyrolysis [18], a microwave–assisted method [19], or vector synthesis [20], hydrothermal [21–23]. The chemical oxidation method is effective, with the possibility of mass production, as it does not need any uncommon tools and can be carried out with a diversity of simple and accessible carbon sources. Candle gray ash [24] and phenolic resin [25] are among the many carbon sources which can be used in this method. For this study, we used citric acid and urea as an inexpensive carbon source for CQD preparation. The water–soluble CQDs prepared via the hydrothermal method have outstanding fluorescence attributes, acceptable stability and high quantum yield. The synthesized CQDs were characterized and assessed and their spectral attributes were investigated.

Bladder cancer (BC) is amongst the most common malignancies occurring in the male urinary system. A rapid increase in BC incidence has been reported in the past years. As with all cancers, early diagnosis is the key to hinder the growth of the tumor. The standard diagnostic procedure used for the detection of BC today is cystoscopy [26], which involves traumatic inspection that can lead to infections in the urinary tract in patients. Furthermore, BC has a high recurrence rate, so it is necessary for patients to be under long–term observation. For these reasons and more, an ideal early diagnosis method with the advantage of operating easily, being non–invasive, highly sensitive, specific, and requiring low cost is a necessity. In recent years, nuclear matrix protein 22 (NMP22) has been the focus of many studies as a potential and novel biomarker for BC [27–29]. Detection of urinary NMP22 has ensured a sensitivity of 100% and specificity of 95% and provides a simple, non–invasive method, making it highly appropriate for the diagnosis and prognosis of BC with high rates of relapse. Even though ELISA methods have adequate sensitivity for NMP22 detection, they have many drawbacks including the need for a series of cumbersome immune reactions, being highly time–consuming, and the inability to be used for large–scale, rapid detection. Electrochemical immunoassay, which combines immune techniques with electrochemical detection, is an efficient clinical detection procedure [30] ensuring high sensitivity and specificity and requiring less time and lower cost. An electrochemical immunosensor is able to rapidly detect NMP22 in the urine samples of BC patients [31].

In this work, the fluorescence of nitrogen doped carbon dots is enhanced due to the bonding to anti–NMP 22 through the EDC/NHS protocol. The fluorescence of the bio–conjugated carbon dot with the monoclonal antibody (mAb) decreases when a series of NMP22 solution with different concentrations were added the bio–conjugated NCDs –mAb. The fluorescence decreased with increasing concentrations of NMP22 as shown in scheme 1 . As fluorescence intensity values decrease differently, a new fluorescence immunosensor was generated to determine the concentrations of NMP22. This study can be the first step for the use of fluorescent NCDs for NMP22 detection in low concentrations and ease the detection of bladder cancer in early stages and in short time.

Citric acid, urea, 1–Ethyl–3–(3–dimethylaminopropyl)–carbodiimide (EDC) and N–hydroxysulfoxuccinimide (sulfo–NHS) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. The NMP22 antibody (anti–NMP 22) and NMP22 were attained from DNA biotech Co. (Tehran, Iran, http://dnabiotech.ir/). Bovine serum albumin (BSA) was acquired from TAKARA Bio Inc. (http://www.takara–bio.com/). Ultrapure water was used in all synthesis procedures. All reagents were of scientific reagent grade, and used as received without further purification.

The preparation of the NCDs was done according to the reported method [32]. First, amounts of 0.5 urea g and 1 g of citric acid were dissolved in doubly distilled water (15 mL). The acquired solution was then transferred to a 25 mL Teflon–lined stainless–steel autoclave in order to heat it at 160C for 6 h. After the heating process, the reactors should be cooled to room temperature. The resulting brown and transparent product was centrifuged for 10 min in 10,000 rpm and then filtered using a 25 µ filter membrane, and then at the end dialyzed against doubly distilled water for 24 h to acquire the NCDs. Finally, the prepared NCDs were stored at 4C to be used later.

In order to measure the quantum yield (ɸ) of NCDs and the conjugates, the integrated photoluminescence intensities and the absorbency values of the NCDs were compared with those of the reference quinine sulfate (QS). The quinine sulfate (literature ɸ is equal to 0.54 at 360 nm) was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (refractive index (Ƞ) of 1.33) and the NCDs were dissolved in doubly distilled water (Ƞ = 1.33), as reported by [33].
ϕ =
ϕ R

I R

A R

Where ɸ is the quantum yield, the measured integrated emission intensity symbolled as I, Ƞ is the refractive index, and A is the optical density, respectively. The subscript R refers to the reference fluorophore of known quantum yield.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the NCDs –mAb conjugated reacted with NMP22
ce:section.ce:para: Different volumes from 10 µL to 100 µL of the diluted carbon dot solution 1:4 from the synthesized solution were tested. The best fluorescence emission of carbon dots can be seen when 50 µL of carbon dots is mixed with 50 µL EDC at (10 mg/mL) and 50 µL NHS at (10 mg/mL) in 1 µL of 0.1 M PBS buffer (pH 7) and incubated at 37 °C for 15 min with shaking at 500 rpm. Then, 10 µL of (0.4 mg/mL) the monoclonal antibodies was mixed into the reaction mixture and again, incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. The unreacted functional groups on the surface of carbon dots were blocked by using 2 µL of BSA (1%) solution. Finally, 10 µL of Nuclear Matrix Protein 22 (0.4 mg/mL) was added to the solution and incubated again at 37 °C for 1 h. The fluorescence of the solution was measured at 340 nm.

Human urine samples were attained from two patients from Tehran Hospital. These samples were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove any solid particles and blood in the samples, and were stored at-20 °C for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence immunoassay for human NMP22 assessment
ce:section.ce:para: According to previous procedures, tubes containing various volumes of human NMP22 each with 163 µL NCDs –mAb were mixed into 0.1 M PBS buffer solution (pH 7) with a final volume of 1.3 mL, and the subsequent mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 1 h to allow the NCDs –mAb–human NMP22 immunocomplex to form. Via the fluorescence spectrophotometer and at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm, the fluorescence emission spectra of the immunocomplex were assessed. To quantify the amount of the human NMP22, the ratio of fluorescence change (F0–F)/F0 was employed, in which F0 is the blank Fluorescence intensity without NMP22.

As witnessed under the TEM, the NCDs were spheroidal particles, uniformly dispersed with a diameter of 4 nm Fig. 1 (a). A significant and wide absorption peak Fig. 1(b) was detected in the ultraviolet region and around 347 nm, the characteristic peak was seen. The emission band Fig. 1(b) with noticeable fluorescence intensity was detected around 492 nm with the optimal excitation wavelength at 300 nm, corresponding the NCDs emitted blue fluorescence Scheme 1 under an obscured camera ultraviolet lamp. Using a CHNOS Elemental Analyzer and Energy Dispersive X–ray (EDX) spectra of NCDs Fig. 1(d) shows elemental analysis of the sample was done revealing the nanoparticle composition to be C 63%, O 19% and N 17%. CHNOS elemental analyzer shows the NCD's purity and approved nitrogen doping. The fluorescence quantum yield calculation was done according to the method proposed in the literature [29] and the attained amount was about 44.5% [14]. The surface functional groups of nitrogen doped carbon dot were detected by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra Fig. 1(c). The broad band around 3600––2700 cm−1 is related to the O—H and N—H stretching vibrations and C––H stretching, while the peaks at 1709 cm−1 can be assigned to C==O vibrations. N—H and C—N stretching peaks of amino groups appeared at 1579 cm−1 and 1353 cm−1, respectively [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The conjugation of antibodies on NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Conjugation is an important phase during the FLISA method. As a result, the importance of choosing a proper conjugation procedure is crucial. So far, many conjugation protocols have been proposed [35] namely direct conjugation using active esters, direct conjugation via 4–(N–maleimidomethyl)cyclohexanecarboxylic acid N–hydrosuccinimide ester (SMCC), indirect conjugation with avidin as a bridge protein and biotinylated antibodies, and modified protein G based indirect conjugation. The active esters method is among the first conjugation methods, which is based on EDC and NHS reagents. This procedure has distinctive advantages namely its simple and inexpensive nature and its need of common reagents, and is commonly carried out in a one–pot reaction. This procedure has been extensively used by many researchers for QDs and antibody conjugation, such as QDs to specific antibodies against morphine [36], quinoxaline–2–carboxylic acid, methyl–3–quinoxaline–2–carboxylic acid [37], ochratoxin A [38,39], etc. In reference to the aforementioned notes, in this study the active esters method was chosen to create QD–mAb conjugates. The carboxylate groups of monoclonal antibodies can conjugate with carboxylate on the surface of carbon dot by the EDC coupling method. As displayed in Fig. 2 (a), the FTIR spectra of NCDs –mAb and mAb demonstrated typical amide bond peaks at 1657 cm−1 (C = O amide I) and 1479 cm−1 (N–C = O amide II), while only the C = O stretch was seen for the NCDs, which might be due to the presence of modified carboxyl group in the absence of the amide II signal. The Bradford protein assay is applied for calculating the binding efficiency between NCDs and mAb. After NCDs conjugated to mAb the solution is centrifuged at 10,000 rpm. The solution is filtrated then bending efficiency was calculated and it was equal to 75%. The success of the covalent coupling between monoclonal antibody and NCDs can be proved by the presence of amide linkages on the NCDs –mAbs. A max emission wavelength of 492 nm (λ ex=300 nm) was seen in fluorescence spectra of the conjugates, which can also be seen for NCDs, indicating that the profound optical properties of QDs coupled with mAbs were intact Fig. 2(b). The fluorescence intensity of NCDs –mAb was higher than NCDs because of the formation of an amide bond between NCDs and antibody. Some studies have demonstrated that nitrogen affects the electronic properties by partaking in the core structure in different ways. [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimal conditions for human NMP22 detection
ce:section.ce:para: The concentrations of NCDs –mAb have a discernable effect on the immunoassay efficiency. The impact of NCDs –mAb dosage on the system was examined in order to reach a wide linear range and high sensitivity 3(a). The results reveal that there isn't a shift in the peak at 492 nm but enhanced fluorescence intensity could be witnessed at higher concentrations of mAb. Conversely, as the concentration of NCDs –mAb increased over the limit of 0.184 mg/mL, it was witnessed that the increasing tendency turned to a platform. As a result, 0.184 mg/mL of NCDs –mAb was used in further experiments. Because of the stability of the immunocomplexes in PBS, the antigen–antibody immunoreactions were mostly carried out in this buffer and 0.1 M PBS was chosen as the reaction medium. As can be seen in Fig. 3 (b), no significant change in fluorescence intensity was seen in solutions with pH varying from 5.0 to 9.0. Hence, for the preservation of the antibody activity, the entire procedure was performed at 37 °C for 30 min in 0.1 M PBS of pH 7.4. The reaction stability is shown in Fig. 3(c) [33].

We established an immunosensing technique based on NCDs for the detection of human NMP22. The detection performance of this sensor was assessed by mixing different concentrations of NMP22 with NCDs –mAbs under optimal conditions (optimum concentration of NCDs–mAb was 0.184 mg/mL, pH = 7.4 and incubation time was 30 min at 37 °C.) As shown in Fig. 4 , introduction of NMP22 to NCDs–mAbs resulted in a significant fluorescence quenching of the system without spectral shift when compared to the fluorescence emission of NCDs –mAb. Moreover, the fluorescence intensities of NCDs –mAbs decreased monotonically as the amount of NMP22 increased. To measure the human NMP22, a calibration curve Fig. 1S was developed using a selection of NMP22 standard solutions at various concentrations. An acceptable linear correlation between the fluorescence intensity ratio and NMP22 concentrations in the range of 0.2––16.3 ng/mL as demonstrated by the curve as evident in Fig. 1S. The linear equation was y = 0.26 + 0.20 × and R 2= 0.99. The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.047 ng/mL (47 pg/mL) which is comparable and even lower than ones of numerous reported immunoassay techniques for NMP22 as can be seen in Table 1 . The obtained relative standard deviation (R.S.D.) was 1.30%, 1.33% and 2.54% for 1.6, 5.5 and 16.3 ng/mL of NMP22, respectively, demonstrating an excellent precision of the fluorescent probe.

By testing the response of the fluorescent probe to multiple coexisting substances (BSA, Glucose, Vitamin C and Cytochrome C),There is not any effect of urine sample that is used on our NCDs. The selectivity of the fluorescent probe under the optimal conditions and in the presence of 3 ng/mL of NMP22 explained in Table 2 . If the relative error (Er) surpasses the threshold of ±5%, the coexisting substance will be considered as an interfering agent. It was found that most substances only caused negligible fluorescence change at considerably high concentrations. The results confirmed the outstanding selectivity of this fluorescent probe for NMP22 detection.

To evaluate the potential of this fluorescent probe for real sample analysis, urine obtained from a local hospital in Tehran, Iran were utilized. Four samples of patients diagnosed with bladder cancer were used in this experiment [43]. The analytical application of the fluorescence was verified by detecting the recoveries at different concentrations (0.160, 01.305, 1.515 and 0.960 ng/mL) of NMP22 in the urine samples. Each specimen was first pre–treated and then filtrated using Whatman filter paper in order to remove any solid particles. The filtrated urine solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The recovery was 96.50––103.61% and the RSD was in the range of 0.90–4.85% Table 3 . These results indicate that the developed sensor holds great potential and can be a reliable method for NMP22 determination in urine samples.

In conclusion, NCDs with a quantum yield as high as 44.5% were synthesized that have carboxylic group and surface passivated amine group. Then, the carboxylated NCDs were complexed with antibody mAb and NCDs –labeled mAb conjugates were developed. With the addition of NMP 22 to NCDs –mAb, a fluorescence immunoassay approach was created, which can be used for the quantitative assessments of the human NMP22. As indicated by the results, the suggested method has significant performance for human NMP22 sensing with a wide linear range and low limit of detection. Furthermore, NMP22 concentrations in human urine can be assessed with acceptable results. This simple and direct procedure is expected to advocate the application of NCDs–based sensors in fluorescence immunoassays and lead to novel developments in immunofluorescence techniques.

Hazha Omar Othman and Foad Salehnia designed and performed the experiments and also wrote the manuscript. Morteza Hosseini as the corresponding author designed experiments, sponsored the study and made all the arrangements. He also edited the manuscript. Rebwar Hassan, Azad Faizullah and Mohammad Reza Ganjali also edited the manuscript. All the authors reviewed the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was sponsored by the research council of the University of Tehran, Salahaddin University-Erbil and Iran National Science Foundation (INSF 98000848). The authors are grateful for their commercial support.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.microc.2020.104966.
